speaker: interviewer
text: July 13, 1995. Tape number four. You were telling us you were at Braunschweig. It's the winter-- December of 1945.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were coming into this house where I was saying my sister saved my life again.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And many times we would run into a cellar to see if you could find some food there. So in one of the places we find some nice black potato burned from the-- the bombing. And we were eating that. It was the most delicious things-- food we could ever have. That was our delicacy over there. And then when we finished over there, it was time to move on again.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had cleaned the rubble. What do you--

speaker: interviewee
text: We cleaned-- we cleaned the rubble over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Moved rocks. You moved rocks?

speaker: interviewee
text: With shovels-- whatever we could do. I mean, not the very, very heavy rocks, but whatever we could do, we did. I-- I think they just wanted to keep us busy, to do something. I think they needed a bulldozer there to really clean up the rubble. But I remember one church was standing there in-- in pretty good shape. But most of the-- the city was all-- all bombed. And so that wasn't really a camp. It was just a place where we stayed and worked. And from there we were moving on to another place. The name was called Bendorf.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get to Bendorf?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only with the cattle cars-- all the time-- all the time. We were locked up. We-- we weren't getting loose any time. We were watched very, very careful. The SS men with their guns and the German shepherd dog was always around us. I mean, there was-- there was-- at that time, it wasn't wired-- electric wire fence around us, but we wouldn't even think of it, to escape anyplace. Because I don't think we could have gone too far to be not recognized who we were. And so Bendorf was a German city, too. We really didn't stay in the city. We had just a big, big room there where we were. And over there, too-- you see, we didn't know this already, at the whole end we came near the war because we had no way to get in touch with anybody or hear any news. But it just so happened, some of the camp already-- perhaps, it was already liberated. Like I'm sure already-- of course, Auschwitz Birkenau was liberated early, and many others. But in this Bendorf here, they made us work in a-- a salt mine. I remember they were taking us down with a freight train. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: You stayed outside of the city of Bendorf in a-- in a--

speaker: interviewee
text: Just in a building.

speaker: interviewer
text: In a building.

speaker: interviewee
text: In a building.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then they put you on a train?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. We were walking to the-- we were marching-- not walking, but marching-- like soldiers. Yes. And we were riding-- they took us down-- from outside, you think it's nothing there, but it was actually a salt mine. And they took us down with a-- with a freight elevator. And then we had to walk for miles and miles. And it was very dim light there. And, of course, you know, the mine itself, it wasn't very, very tall, you know? And end of the walk, we came to this beautiful building. You would never--

speaker: interviewer
text: Underground?

speaker: interviewee
text: Underground. You would never thought it was a munition factory there.

speaker: interviewer
text: A what kind of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Munition factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which is ammunition?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ammunition, ammunition, yes. Ammunition. We were working on the guns and the bullets, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Over there-- just assemble. They showed us, you know, what to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't actually work in the salt mine there, you worked in the munitions--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. We just went through this--

speaker: interviewer
text: Through the salt mine.

speaker: interviewee
text: Through the salt mine. This factory was in the salt mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: So from outside, you really didn't know it was a factory there. But over there they had German people, too, who was working. And that was-- that was a hard work, too, because you had to inhale that salt. You get swollen up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's where I lost my would-be sister-in-law, my friend, who was-- we used to go to school together. And she couldn't take it anymore. She just-- that little bit of a food what we used to get, she always used to save. Instead of eating it, she used to save it for the next day. And then somebody would stole it from her so she wouldn't have that either. And we always used to tell her, why don't you eat it? You know, at least you'd have your strength from that little bit of a thing. But we couldn't-- Was there a lot of stealing at that time of food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. You know, sometimes when we would have to-- we would purposely save just a little bit of a piece of-- of-- of bread. But then the other prisoner would come and steal from under your head when you were asleep. And that's all they could steal. We didn't have nothing else-- you know, just food. And for food, you'd be surprised what people would do when you are hungry. It's unbelievable.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, they would, you know, I mean, they would go to steal, and their stomach's would be filled up. I mean, they don't think about the other person. I mean, she was a prisoner, too. I know I couldn't do it myself. I would rather starve than to take somebody else's bread away-- that little portion what we got.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we stayed there for a while, and from there we had a stopover. What-- so what happened? You'd work in the mines.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were working in the mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: In these munition--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. And I guess, as I told you, we didn't know this but the German know that they have to move us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any instances of sabotage?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, they just did what they were supposed to.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Not in this place. No. And see, they know that we couldn't stay there anymore because we-- probably we would have been captured-- maybe we would have been freed sooner. So they wanted to move us on for the Reveille. And after-- we didn't stay there very, very long, but long enough to be underground--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: --in a-- in a salt mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe two, three weeks. I don't think any more than that. I can't give exact dates because, as I told you before, we didn't have a calender, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And from there we had a stopover in a very small camp they called Achsengor. And we only stayed there for two days. And over there we didn't do anything-- just waiting to go someplace else. So we called it the beets lager, because we were fed with beets over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Beads?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beets, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beets. No. No, just the food. You know, the vegetable--

speaker: interviewer
text: Beans?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Beets.

speaker: interviewer
text: Beads.

speaker: interviewee
text: Beets-- the red beets.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, beets.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, beets.

speaker: interviewer
text: Beets. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah. So we named that. You know, that was our favorite lager, because we got food over there. So they told us they going to take us someplace. So here we go again. They put us into this cattle car. They jammed us with the girls. They give us an empty pail-- very little food. We had to be very fast-- everything. And they locked us up. And this was in about middle of April, I would say, maybe. And we were riding, and riding, and no place to go. This was an endless ride. And I remember we could hear again the airplanes, the shooting. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the girls were dying right in front of us. And-- and it was so heavy to breathe, you know, in that closed-in cattle car. And one day-- oh, the-- the train was dancing on the tracks. But they won't open up the door or even look in to see.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean it was dancing on the tracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they were bombing, you know, they were shooting. The airplane was flying over our head, but we couldn't see nothing. But from all this kind of a, you know, thing, the train was, like, jumping on the track. And one day the train stopped, and that was in a forest. And first we didn't know exactly where we were. It wasn't a camp. They opened the door, and they told us to get out. And many of the girls-- they just couldn't move. I mean, they were dead. They stayed there. They made us-- we were so weak by that time, we could hardly stand up ourselves. They made us stand up five in a row, and they were count-- they-- they were still counting us. They were their favorite thing to do-- counting. And they had the machine guns all around us. And they made us lay down. Everybody had to lay down in the same direction. And the German shepherd dog was there. Even if you would want it to run away, you couldn't. And we wouldn't even try. We were too weak. And they made us stand up again. And the airplanes were over our heads-- so many. And we could hear they were very close. Whoever came to our rescue, they were very close-by.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you heard, like--

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we heard shooting and the airplanes, but we only saw that time the SS men who were with us and the German shepherd dogs. And-- and the-- their machine guns were all around us. So my sister said we better say our prayer because they were going to kill us. And that's what they were to do. If they would have had a little more time, they probably would do that. But they must have saw us from above, you know. And-- and God was with us, too. And all of a sudden, we see these SS men disappear one by one.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were all standing there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were standing there, yes. And they leave the machine guns there in position. And as they left, we were all alone but not long. And when we heard truckloads of soldiers were coming, and motorcycle was coming with soldiers. And we saw they were wearing Red Cross armband on their uniform. So we know something was happened, but yet, we didn't know exactly what. And then they came to us, and-- and they told us that the war was over.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what language did they speak?

speaker: interviewee
text: And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what language?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the International Red Cross or the Swedish Red Cross. I'm not sure. I'm-- I'm-- I'm not sure of that because I personally, I didn't, you know, talk to either of them. But they were able to explain to us that the war was over, and they are here to help us. And we didn't know-- we didn't know what to do. Be happy-- of course, we were happy because we stayed alive. But then our eyes opened up for a nightmare-- what we went through. We lost our family. We lost our friends. They were all killed. But these wonderful people--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your parents were killed? And your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody. By having the Auschwitz album, I have a picture of her and my-- she's holding my little cousin who was her niece. And they're standing right in front of the crematorium. And her sister with her-- with her four children was there, too. And then I find another page where my father is-- were selected where he's with my cousin.

speaker: interviewer
text: But in this group, it was just your sister and yourself that--

speaker: interviewee
text: That group were when we arrived, it was my sister, my aunt--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, when you were in Buchenwald Forest when you were-- who survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that-- that's not in the book.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no. But who survived that part?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my sister, and the aunt of my would-be sister-in-law-- only the three of us. Yeah. My Aunt Rose, they-- she got killed. And my future sister-in-law, she, you know, died.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the Red Cross came, what did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the Red Cross came, then the first thing they did is they give us sugar, and a little piece of bread-- of course, much better one what we used to-- and water. That was our first meal after two weeks. And then they put us back to the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: The regular train?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- no. They-- they-- they cleaned out the train first, what we been. You know, because this was in the forest. They didn't have any other train there. And just so many people they put in one of those cattle train. And they didn't lock the doors anymore. And they took us into-- I think Germany. We went through Germany, and they-- over there they put us into a regular train. And they took us to Denmark first, and we stayed there for two days in Denmark.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where in Denmark? Do you-- do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Copenhagen. And over there, they-- they treated us so good. And from there, they took us with a small boat over to Sweden. The Swedish government took in the prisoner. I could only say the nicest thing about those Swedish people, you know? They cleaned us up. They took away all--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in Sweden or in Denmark that they cleaned you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this was in Sweden. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what city?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Malmo.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: M-A-L-M-O. I don't know what word to go by.

speaker: interviewer
text: You've got it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Malmo. Yeah. And over there, they took our dirty clothes away. They cleaned us up from the lice. They kept us in quarantine for at least six weeks. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] again-- you know, I mean, there was-- I mean, we were just so sick and very fragile, you know, especially my sister. She got frostbite on her leg and her hand. And I-- I could really-- I could really-- when I look at her, you know, I could really thank her.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were a great support-- one to the other.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was wonderful. And in Sweden, again, I was young, you know. So again, I was separated from my sister. She went with the older groups, and we young girls-- they put us into a girls school. They took us-- after the quarantine, we were taken to Hasselby slott. That was near Stockholm.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know about that. Hasselby slott. Well, anyways in Sweden near Stockholm, it was an empty castle and they let-- let us have it. And they really treated us well over there. They taught us Swedish. They taught us Hungarian. They taught us all kind of a things-- cooking, you know, cleaning. Whatever chore we had to do, it-- it was just marvelous, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we just wanted to forget about everything what we went through. We didn't want to talk about it. We didn't want to think about it. We just wanted to live, you know? Except our prisoners, you know, our girls-- when we were all alone and we got together, then we would just, you know, talk about our experience, what we went through. But after the school-- I was there for a whole year with the girls. Did they teach you a vocation or anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, no, not there. But I always wanted to be a hairstylist. I like to work with hair. So after the school, a lot of girls went back to Hungary. Some of them came here to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Sweden?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, in Hasselby slott.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Hasselby slott and running, I was there a year from 1945 till 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were in two places?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two places-- yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: They moved you from one.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, because one of-- in the slott, it was a very big place. And then when a lot of girls left, then we really could use a smaller place. So it was like three villas-- three house separately. And It was a beautiful place in between the mountains. And-- and they really dressed us up. They gave us a-- a suitcase. They even give us spending money for every week, because we didn't have nothing-- absolutely nothing. And from when the school was over, I have the name of the girls. One of the girls made me a beautiful go away present, you know, put every girl's name on it. And from there, I told my sister, you know, she should come up near Stockholm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you were able to communicate with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yeah. Oh, yeah. We went to visit the other group, sure. I saw her, and I talked to her on the phone. We wrote to each other. And so she and another friend came up near Stockholm, and that's where we made our home. She find a job to do sewing. And after I finished the school, then I went over there, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to stay there up to a certain age? Is that how they did it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, because-- yeah, yes. Because, you see, we weren't ready to go out to-- to work yet at that time. So I went to the same factory where she was working. And she know how much I wanted to be a hairstylist. So she said to me, she going to work, and I should go to school-- hairdressing school. So that's what I did. I went to hairdressing school in Stockholm. And I learned a trade over there. And she was working. And then after I got my diploma and a license, then I went to work in a beauty parlor. And we set up a home. We had a nice little apartment. We shared-- three of us, three girls-- we shared one-- one big room. Then in the other room was a Polish couple-- survivors-- were living. But they were working in the same place where-- where my sister did. And we shared a kitchen and a bathroom. But was-- it was nice, a very nice place.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the-- the Swedish people were so trusting, you know? I-- I learned how to ride a bicycle. They would give me a bicycle to take to ride into to the city-- two years. You know, they didn't-- they know I'm going to bring it back. Children were waiting for me when-- the time in the evening. You know, they were all front of the door to greet me when I was coming home from work. And they were-- we made a lot of friends there in Sweden-- Swedish people and, of course, you know, other people.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were living there five years. We learned the language. I spoke very well Swedish when I came over here to the United States. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when I came here, you know, I wanted to learn the English language.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened with your brothers while you were in Sweden?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, you see, my-- we find our younger brother through the Red Cross who survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mayer. And he went back to Hungary, and he didn't find nobody there except his sweetheart, who they got married later, you know. And they lived there not too long in Hungary. They moved back to his wife's place in [NON-ENGLISH], because that's where they had the bakery. And later on they-- they escaped to Italy. And then our relatives were looking for us, too, through the Red Cross. They find us in Sweden. We find each other, so we were corresponding. And they were sending affidavits for us to come here to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Mayer? What happened to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mayer, he was in Italy yet. But then in 1947, he took the boat, and he went to Palestine-- it wasn't Israel yet-- and they were captured. He was captured, he was taken on to Cyprus. And his wife and the baby, he was taken some other place. And after a while, we didn't hear from him for a-- quite a while. But then, he got reunited with his wife and the baby, and they find a place over there in-- in--

speaker: interviewer
text: In Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Israel and live. And then we started corresponding from Sweden to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had relatives in the States?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had relatives here, so we wanted to come here to America. But because we had to wait so long for our affidavit, we told him that we going to come to Israel. And he was the happiest one. We're going to be together. Oh, gee, it was-- that's how we survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
